title: VM Density Guide
summary: Understand host cores, host memory, reserved capacity, per-VM CPU/RAM allocations, oversubscription, overcommit, and spare capacity before consolidating workloads.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether a virtualization host can support the planned VM profile with practical CPU and RAM headroom.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning virtualization hosts, small clusters, lab environments, server consolidation, workload expansion, or host refresh decisions.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible VM Density Estimator inputs and explain how each value affects consolidation pressure.

items.label: Host CPU Cores
items.description: The physical CPU core capacity available on the host.
items.whyItMatters: Core capacity limits how much CPU work the host can schedule without contention.
items.commonMistake: Counting logical threads or marketing specs without checking usable physical-core capacity.

items.label: Host RAM (GB)
items.description: The installed memory available on the host before reserves and VM allocations are considered.
items.whyItMatters: RAM is often the first hard density ceiling because VMs need committed memory to stay stable.
items.commonMistake: Assuming CPU is the only density limiter.

items.label: Reserved (GB)
items.description: Memory held back for the host OS, hypervisor, management services, and operational safety.
items.whyItMatters: Reserved memory prevents the host itself from competing with the guest workloads.
items.commonMistake: Packing VMs into memory that the host still needs for stable operation.

items.label: VM vCPU
items.description: The number of virtual CPUs assigned to each VM in the modeled workload profile.
items.whyItMatters: Per-VM vCPU allocation drives scheduler pressure and affects how many VMs can fit comfortably.
items.commonMistake: Giving every VM more vCPU than it actually needs.

items.label: VM RAM (GB)
items.description: The amount of RAM assigned to each modeled VM.
items.whyItMatters: VM RAM allocation often controls the final density limit more than CPU allocation.
items.commonMistake: Using a template value that is too large or too small for the real workload.

items.label: CPU Oversubscription
items.description: The allowed ratio of assigned vCPU to physical CPU capacity.
examples: Higher ratios for very light workloads
items.whyItMatters: Oversubscription can improve consolidation but raises contention risk when workloads are active together.
items.commonMistake: Assuming high oversubscription is safe without understanding workload peaks.

items.label: RAM Overcommit
items.description: Whether assigned VM memory is allowed to exceed physical memory assumptions.
items.whyItMatters: Memory overcommit can raise density, but it can also create paging and instability when workloads grow.
items.commonMistake: Treating RAM overcommit as free capacity.

items.label: Spare (%)
items.description: The percentage of host capacity reserved for bursts, maintenance, failover, and growth.
items.whyItMatters: Spare capacity keeps the host from running at the edge and supports operational events.
items.commonMistake: Filling the host because the calculator says one more VM technically fits.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means VM density appears reasonable. Watch means CPU, RAM, overcommit, or spare assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the host may become overloaded, difficult to maintain, or unable to tolerate bursts or failover.
bullets: The limiting side may be CPU or RAM, not necessarily both.
Oversubscription and overcommit are policy decisions, not guarantees.
Spare capacity is part of the design, not leftover waste.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: VM Density uses the CPU, RAM, and storage context built earlier in the Compute flow to test whether the platform can actually consolidate workloads safely.
bullets: Do not use VM count alone as a density measure.
Keep CPU and RAM assumptions consistent with prior steps.
Use density results before considering GPU acceleration or power/thermal impact.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
